summary: Exodus 24:10 presents one of the Bible's most anthropomorphic visions—the elders 'saw the God of Israel' with 'under his feet' a pavement of sapphire. The ancient versions systematically diminish this stark theophanic language through pronominal distancing (Targum), insertion of mediating glory-terminology (LXX, Targum), and interpretive buffering that protects divine transcendence while preserving the narrative framework.
summary_plain: The original Hebrew describes Israelite elders literally seeing God and the ground under his feet. Later translations softened this shocking image by adding words like 'glory' or changing pronouns to avoid saying people saw God directly, protecting the belief that God has no physical body.

revisions.mt_reading: וַיִּרְאוּ אֵת אֱלֹהֵי יִשְׂרָאֵל ('and they saw the God of Israel')
revisions.revised_reading: וַחֲזוֹ יָת יְקָרָא דַאֲלָהָא דְיִשְׂרָאֵל ('and they saw the glory of the God of Israel')
revisions.evidence: Targum Onqelos inserts יְקָרָא (yeqara, 'glory') as a mediating buffer between the human subject and the divine object. This is a consistent Targumic strategy documented throughout theophanies (cf. Ex 33:18-23, Num 12:8). The insertion is theologically motivated rather than linguistically required, since את can take a direct object without intermediary.
revisions.evidence_plain: The Aramaic translation adds the word 'glory' so readers understand the elders saw God's glory, not God himself—a classic rabbinic way to protect God's invisibility.
scholarly_citations: Smelik, The Targum of Judges, pp. 52-54
Levey, The Messiah: An Aramaic Interpretation, pp. 8-11
revisions.counter_arguments: One could argue that 'glory' represents legitimate interpretive translation of what 'seeing God' phenomenologically meant in ancient Israelite experience, not revision.

revisions.mt_reading: וַיִּרְאוּ אֵת אֱלֹהֵי יִשְׂרָאֵל ('and they saw the God of Israel')
revisions.revised_reading: καὶ εἶδον τὸν τόπον οὗ εἱστήκει ἐκεῖ ὁ θεὸς τοῦ Ισραηλ ('and they saw the place where the God of Israel stood')
revisions.evidence: The LXX radically restructures the clause to avoid the direct vision of God. Instead of seeing God himself, the elders see 'the place where...God stood'—an intentional distancing that substitutes location for person. This aligns with Hellenistic Jewish philosophical concerns about divine incorporeality and transcendence, well-attested in Philo and later rabbinic sources (cf. mSotah 5:2 on God 'standing').
revisions.evidence_plain: The Greek translation changes 'they saw God' to 'they saw the place where God stood,' avoiding the claim that humans saw God's actual form.
scholarly_citations: Wevers, Notes on the Greek Text of Exodus, pp. 389-390
Rösel, Übersetzung als Vollendung der Auslegung, pp. 112-115
revisions.counter_arguments: The LXX may reflect a variant Vorlage that already contained מָקוֹם, though no Hebrew manuscript evidence supports this, making it more likely a free rendering.

revisions.revised_reading: וּתְחוֹת כֻּרְסֵי יְקָרֵהּ ('and under the throne of his glory')
revisions.evidence: Targum Onqelos replaces the direct anthropomorphism רַגְלָיו ('his feet') with כֻּרְסֵי יְקָרֵהּ ('throne of his glory'), a double distancing mechanism: (1) substituting throne for body part, and (2) adding 'glory' as further buffer. This reflects systematic Targumic policy against divine body language, part of a broader halakhic-exegetical tradition. Neofiti and Ps-Jonathan follow similar strategies.
revisions.evidence_plain: Instead of 'under his feet,' the Aramaic says 'under his glorious throne,' removing the image of God having feet entirely.
scholarly_citations: Hayward, Divine Name and Presence: The Memra, pp. 45-48
Grossfeld, The Targum Onqelos to Exodus, pp. 71-72
Chester, Divine Revelation and Divine Titles, pp. 289-291
revisions.counter_arguments: The throne imagery may represent legitimate ancient Near Eastern royal theophany convention rather than revisionist theology, though the consistency across Targumim suggests intentional policy.

revisions.revised_reading: καὶ τὰ ὑπὸ τοὺς πόδας αὐτοῦ ('and the things under his feet')
revisions.evidence: The LXX retains πόδας ('feet') but subtly shifts focus through τὰ ὑπό ('the things under'), emphasizing the pavement rather than the divine anatomy. While less aggressive than the Targum, this represents mild buffering—readers attend to the sapphire-work, not the feet themselves. The plural neuter article τά creates grammatical distance.
revisions.evidence_plain: The Greek keeps the word 'feet' but phrases it to draw attention to what's under the feet (the sapphire pavement) rather than the feet themselves.
scholarly_citations: Wevers, Notes on the Greek Text of Exodus, pp. 390-391
Le Boulluec and Sandevoir, L'Exode, pp. 232-233
revisions.counter_arguments: This may simply be literal translation of the Hebrew construction without theological motivation, as the LXX elsewhere translates divine anthropomorphisms straightforwardly (e.g., Ex 15:6).

revisions.mt_reading: וַיִּרְאוּ אֵת אֱלֹהֵי יִשְׂרָאֵל וְתַחַת רַגְלָיו ('they saw the God of Israel, and under his feet')
revisions.revised_reading: ܘܚܙܘ ܠܐܠܗܐ ܕܐܝܣܪܐܝܠ ܘܬܚܝܬ ܪܓܠܘܗܝ ('and they saw the God of Israel and under his feet')
revisions.evidence: The Peshitta largely follows MT word-for-word, including the anthropomorphic 'feet' (ܪܓܠܘܗܝ, reglawhy). However, some manuscripts show variant readings with interpretive expansions similar to Targumic tradition, suggesting later scribal discomfort. The base Peshitta text represents a more conservative translation philosophy, though theological revision may appear in textual transmission history.
revisions.evidence_plain: The Syriac translation mostly keeps the original wording about seeing God and his feet, though some later copyists added explanatory words similar to the Aramaic tradition.
scholarly_citations: Weitzman, The Syriac Version of the Old Testament, pp. 78-81
Dirksen, The Transmission of the Text in the Peshitta Manuscripts, pp. 34-36
revisions.counter_arguments: The straightforward rendering may indicate the Peshitta translators did not share the same theological anxieties as LXX or Targum tradents, or worked from a different exegetical tradition.

revisions.mt_reading: וַיִּרְאוּ אֵת אֱלֹהֵי יִשְׂרָאֵל וְתַחַת רַגְלָיו ('they saw the God of Israel, and under his feet')
revisions.revised_reading: et viderunt Deum Israhel et sub pedibus eius ('and they saw the God of Israel and under his feet')
revisions.evidence: Jerome's Vulgate renders the text with remarkable literalism, preserving both 'saw God' (viderunt Deum) and 'feet' (pedibus). However, Jerome elsewhere shows awareness of Jewish interpretive traditions and philosophical theology (cf. his correspondence with Augustine on anthropomorphisms). The retention here may reflect intentional adherence to Hebrew text against LXX, privileging textual fidelity over theological revision—itself a theological choice in the context of late ancient debates.
revisions.evidence_plain: The Latin translation by Jerome keeps the direct language about seeing God and his feet, choosing literal accuracy over theological smoothing.
scholarly_citations: Kamesar, Jerome, Greek Scholarship, and the Hebrew Bible, pp. 45-47
revisions.counter_arguments: Jerome's literalism reflects translation method rather than theological stance; his commentaries show he understood these passages non-literally despite translating them literally.

revisions.mt_reading: וּכְעֶצֶם הַשָּׁמַיִם לָטֹהַר ('and like the substance/bone of heaven in purity/clarity')
revisions.revised_reading: וּכְגַוַּן אֶבֶן טָבָא וּכְחֵזוּ רְקִיעָא דִשְׁמַיָּא בְּדַכְיוּ ('and like the appearance of precious stone and like the vision of the firmament of heaven in purity')
revisions.evidence: The Targum expands and clarifies the cryptic Hebrew phrase עֶצֶם הַשָּׁמַיִם by adding 'precious stone' (אֶבֶן טָבָא) and 'firmament' (רְקִיעָא), harmonizing with Ezekiel 1:22-26's throne vision vocabulary. This represents proto-rabbinic merkavah exegesis, linking Sinai theophany with Ezekielian temple-chariot imagery into a coherent visionary tradition.
revisions.evidence_plain: The Aramaic adds explanatory words about 'precious stone' and 'firmament' to connect this vision with the similar vision in Ezekiel chapter 1, creating theological consistency.
scholarly_citations: Alexander, The Targum of Canticles, pp. 89-91
revisions.counter_arguments: The expansion may simply clarify obscure Hebrew vocabulary rather than harmonize theological traditions, though the specific lexical choices suggest conscious intertextual linkage.

dominant_strategy: Anthropomorphism avoidance is overwhelmingly dominant across LXX and Targum traditions. Both employ distinct strategies—LXX substitutes location for person, Targum inserts glory/throne mediators—but share the goal of protecting divine transcendence against the stark MT claim that humans 'saw God' with visible feet. This reflects Second Temple period theological development toward divine incorporeality.
overall_assessment: The textual history of Exodus 24:10 reveals systematic Second Temple theological anxiety about the MT's unmediated theophanic vision. LXX and Targum tradents, working independently but within overlapping Hellenistic-Jewish intellectual contexts, deployed complementary revision strategies: spatial distancing (LXX 'place where God stood') and hypostatic buffering (Targum 'glory/throne'). These revisions expose the philosophical-theological shift from ANE anthropomorphic God-language toward transcendentalist models compatible with Greek metaphysics and proto-rabbinic theology. The Peshitta and Vulgate's relative conservatism highlights that revision was not universal but tradition-specific. This passage became a locus classicus for medieval debates on prophecy and divine vision (Maimonides, Guide 1.5).
overall_plain: Later translators were deeply uncomfortable with the idea that people could see God and his feet. Greek and Aramaic translations changed the text in different ways to protect God's invisibility and spiritual nature. This shows how Jewish theology evolved from earlier, more physical descriptions of God toward emphasizing that God has no body.

bibcrit_assessment.title: Theophanic Vision Systematically Mediated in Second Temple Versions
bibcrit_assessment.reasoning: The most significant finding is the convergent yet independent revision strategies in LXX and Targum, demonstrating that diverse Second Temple Jewish communities shared theological discomfort with unmediated divine vision. This pattern suggests Exodus 24:10's anthropomorphic directness preserves genuinely archaic Israelite theology, later deemed problematic. The revisions are not mere translation variants but represent a paradigm shift in theological anthropology.
bibcrit_assessment.plain: The changes show that by the time of Jesus, Jewish translators across different traditions all agreed the original text was too direct about seeing God, revealing a major theological shift in how Jews understood God's nature.
